title: [Proposed feature] Copy an image to a directory using the docker registry v2 structure.
body:
I have this weird use case where I want to host a handful of docker images on a website without running a real docker registry.
My current idea:
- copy the files of the image to a directory structure (i.e. `skopeo copy ... dir:./here`)
- move those files into the docker registry api v2 directory structure
- throw them all into a the static hosting location (like a simple Nginx or exposed Google Storage bucket)
- configure the correct response headers (like `Content-Type`, `Docker-Content-Digest` and `Docker-Distribution-API-Version`) for each file

Given the current `skopeo copy ... dir:./here` I would need to write additional scripting to move everything into the right directories and determine which http response headers are needed. This is doable and also something I suspect more people would want.

My proposal for your consideration:

Add a new target location `repodir` which does essentially the same as `dir` but does 2 things different:
- Put all the files into directory structure that matches the docker registry v2 api.
- Create a machine readable file (json) that specifies the required set of http response headers for each of the generated files. I think this should have a name that allows having multiple images in the same directory structure and not overwrite any existing ones.

So in the end I can do something like this
- `skopeo copy ... repodir:skopeo:latest:/var/www/html/`

From there I can do a find for the filename pattern (`headers-<tagname>.json` ??) that contain all http headers and generate my specific config from there.

comments.body:
some prior related topic https://github.com/containers/skopeo/issues/2490 which links to https://github.com/NicolasT/static-container-registry

I guess the consensus from @mtrmac is still the same that having this directly in skopeo would not make sense

I think the existence of that software outside of this repository is _fine_; I don’t see a strong reason to integrate it natively.

Longer-term, I’m a bit dubious about the practicality of such static servers; e.g. the `/v2/<name>/referrers/<digest>?artifactType=<artifactType>` endpoint is probably _possible_ to implement using a static configuration but “depending on `artifactType`, serve one of 5 possible contents, or a fallback one on no match” is stretching the concept of a “static server” a bit, already.

(Note that the proposal in #2490 is not just “static registry hosting”, at least eventually it swerved into something clearly impractical. But the discussion does already highlight a list of known pain points.)
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

Given the existence of a solution already, and

> dubious about the practicality of such static servers

I don’t think this is worth tracking as an open roadmap item.
